title: The Defense Industrial Alliance Washington Is Throwing Away
gist: Two Canadian political scientists argue that Trump-era coercion is backfiring on U.S. defense-industrial goals: instead of forcing Canada to integrate, it is driving Ottawa to hedge, diversify toward Europe and the Indo-Pacific, and build sovereign 'strategic stack' capabilities, eroding the political trust that defense-industrial cooperation requires.

insights.claim: The Pentagon's unilateral suspension of the 86-year-old Permanent Joint Board on Defense, over Ottawa's lack of a credible plan to spend 3.5 percent of GDP on defense by 2035, is producing the opposite of its intended effect.
insights.speaker: Justin Massie and Srdjan Vucetic
insights.so_what: Signals deterioration in U.S.-Canada defense institutions, a risk to integrated North American supply chains and NORAD.
insights.quote: their actions are producing the opposite of their intended effect
insights.locator: What many policymakers in Washington fail to realize

insights.claim: Effective defense-industrial integration depends on credible demand signals, which are impossible to sustain when one partner can unilaterally redefine priorities and punish perceived noncompliance.
insights.speaker: Justin Massie and Srdjan Vucetic
insights.so_what: Identifies the core structural reason coercion undermines the very industrial cooperation Washington wants.
insights.quote: effective industrial integration depends on credible demand signals, which are difficult to sustain when one partner can unilaterally redefine priorities

insights.claim: The Carney government has launched a nation-building strategy of retaliatory tariffs, rapid trade and investment diversification away from the U.S., expanded military spending, and a landmark strategic defense partnership with the EU.
insights.speaker: Justin Massie and Srdjan Vucetic
insights.so_what: Concrete shift of Canadian defense and trade orientation toward Europe, redirecting procurement away from U.S. firms.
insights.locator: The Carney government has set out on an ambitious nation-building strategy

insights.claim: Canada is pursuing Canadian-controlled 'strategic stack' capabilities including AI, clean energy, critical minerals, semiconductors, space communications, vaccines, radars, drones, and underwater sensors.
insights.speaker: Justin Massie and Srdjan Vucetic
insights.so_what: Maps the sectors where Canada intends to build sovereign capacity, signaling new domestic demand and reduced U.S. reliance.
insights.quote: “strategic stack” areas, including artificial intelligence, clean energy technologies, critical minerals, semiconductors, space-based communications, vaccines

insights.claim: Canada's drive for autonomy revives Pierre Trudeau's 1970s 'Third Option' and the Cold War concept of 'defense against help,' but is now driven by uncertainty about U.S. reliability rather than fear of economic absorption.
insights.speaker: Justin Massie and Srdjan Vucetic
insights.so_what: Frames Canadian hedging as a durable historical pattern, not a temporary reaction, implying persistence beyond Trump.
insights.locator: Like Pierre Trudeau’s “Third Option” in the 1970s

insights.claim: Nine European nations have decided to join a Paris-led nuclear deterrence initiative, while Japan, South Korea, and Australia are expressing interest in European defense production.
insights.speaker: Justin Massie and Srdjan Vucetic
insights.so_what: Evidence of a wider allied pivot toward European defense ecosystems, a tailwind for European primes and a headwind for U.S. exporters.
insights.quote: nine nations of Europe have decided to join a Paris-led nuclear deterrence initiative
insights.locator: Among American allies, nine nations of Europe

insights.claim: Congressional attempts to write predictability back into law via the FY2027 NDAA are only ex post facto and presume congressional authorization still binds an administration prepared to govern around it.
insights.speaker: Justin Massie and Srdjan Vucetic
insights.so_what: Suggests statutory remedies won't quickly restore allied trust, prolonging the unpredictability discount on U.S. partnership.
insights.quote: The United States has already acquired a reputation as an unpredictable partner, and overcoming this perception will take a very long time.
insights.locator: As a result, while Congressional efforts can offer some policy stability

insights.claim: The authors argue durable cooperation requires long-horizon, contractually binding co-production agreements and state/provincial-level partnerships that are harder to unwind than executive declarations.
insights.speaker: Justin Massie and Srdjan Vucetic
insights.so_what: Points to the contracting structures that could re-anchor cross-border defense-industrial demand despite political volatility.
insights.quote: Long-horizon, contractually binding co-production agreements are harder to unwind than executive declarations
insights.locator: Restoring credible demand signals will therefore require
